title: Propagate CONTAINERS_CONF to conmon
body:
The `CONTAINERS_CONF` environment variable can be used to override the configuration file, which is useful for testing.  However, at the moment this variable is not propagated to conmon.  That means in particular, that conmon can't propagate it back to podman when invoking `--exit-command`.  The mismatch in configuration between the starting and cleaning up podman instances can cause a variety of errors.

This patch also adds two related test cases.  One checks explicitly that the correct `$CONTAINERS_CONF` value appears in conmon's environment.  The other checks for a possible specific impact of this bug: if we use a nonstandard name for the runtime (even if its path is just a regular crun), then the podman container cleanup invoked at container exit will fail. That has the effect of meaning that a container started with `-d --rm` won't be correctly removed once complete.

comments.body:
Great catch @dgibson 
LGTM
/approve
@containers/podman-maintainers PTAL
@edsantiago PTAL at tests

LGTM
once @edsantiago 's concerns are addressed.

Failure looks real:
```
         # # podman-remote [...] rm SHA
         # bbb7cc96e3077f7e7508649e6355dd838fd9bff4e787921d6bd7c78e1292994b
         # #/vvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvv
         # #| FAIL: exit code is 0; expected 1
         # #\^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
```
Should this test be `skip_if_remote`-ed?

> Failure looks real:
> 
> ```
>          # # podman-remote [...] rm SHA
>          # bbb7cc96e3077f7e7508649e6355dd838fd9bff4e787921d6bd7c78e1292994b
>          # #/vvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvvv
>          # #| FAIL: exit code is 0; expected 1
>          # #\^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
> ```
> 
> Should this test be `skip_if_remote`-ed?

I don't think so, although I'm not sure, since I know almost nothing about how podman remote works.

AFAICT failing in the way it appears to means that the `--rm` option really is failing to work, which seems like a real bug, and I doubt it's caused by my code change, merely exposed by the new test.  I don't really know why my change which fixes the local case would not fix the remote case - but, again, I really don't know how remote works.  I don't think I'll be able to debug this on my own.

It's a race condition:
```console
$ while :;do cid=$(bin/podman-remote run -d --rm quay.io/libpod/testimage:20210610 true);bin/podman-remote rm $cid;done
f67da6c90c667f7f53a4f35c88b501661424646b47e3c21ee9f9d876d9854750
8ddffb3e971f6986fef5fd21a57c98fe28bdaa70eb66371cbcf6b69395cc1993
Error: no container with name or ID "ee8a170a78727763a88ccb08319f04899ffe94b7de1a76bea2a33d7d6c47a439" found: no such container
Error: no container with name or ID "021aa341a56f9dfab681307eefc1a832226190277897b1e3b4b40319f76e0531" found: no such container
1efc9f1d56946353c51fc7b420f5de274fca7af5af2cfec495edc33300aba629
82f3cf3d2310b85cb2424701a98bcf956981957d93d76dae23a06556259eedda
825797a3f88cdeabcc55be736aecba25c0cab743044e36260705136cbfca9d01
Error: no container with name or ID "d8cdcc884b0013a1fa87d68fac7de274d16f4bc66c8b8ad14bfc3ae975c538f6" found: no such container
30ca114f4ffab6ac87e3741c37251524bdc207fc1a7a9e13e31cbf56a88a9108
a9e78031c688df5868b0197f01ef3c94f6372b00f786bfeb1236ad32acf792e1
d3709db21ac0e1044dc9748f83b1e03ebc8a60b367024a0746e387d4ea102077
```
...and it's an understandable one, in your test, because there really can't be any timing guarantees here. This race affects both local and remote, it just seems probable that remote is slightly slower.

Proposed solution: I don't know. Maybe `run_podman wait $cid`? I would recommend checking out a broken version of podman (one that demonstrates the bug you're trying to test), then figuring out how to reliably give the `run -d --rm` process time to complete and clean up (or not, in the case of the bug). Then write your test to conform to that.

BTW please also remove the `export` from the second test.

Oh - and `CONTAINERS_CONF` doesn't work over remote anyway (I think), so you probably do want to `skip_if_remote` just like in your first test.

This does not relieve you from fixing the race condition, though: it's a real one, and could conceivably trigger on a real run. It's convenient that podman-remote reproduces it so easily, because that can help you write a robust  wait test.

> Oh - and `CONTAINERS_CONF` doesn't work over remote anyway (I think), so you probably do want to `skip_if_remote` just like in your first test.

Ok, I've done that for now.  Do you know how we could find out definitively if `$CONTAINERS_CONF` is supposed to work over remote?

> This does not relieve you from fixing the race condition, though: it's a real one, and could conceivably trigger on a real run. It's convenient that podman-remote reproduces it so easily, because that can help you write a robust wait test.

Given I have no idea how to configure or invoke remote podman, that doesn't actually help that much.  But, I can force that race to happen easily enough using a `sleep 3` instead of a `true`.  I think I have something solid now, lets see what the CI thinks.
Alas, the CI is now failing on the e2e ginkgo testsuite, for reasons I can't discern :(

The [plugin in containers failure](https://storage.googleapis.com/cirrus-ci-6707778565701632-fcae48/artifacts/containers/podman/5933325356367872/html/int-podman-fedora-35-root-container.log.html#t--use-plugin-in-containers--1) looks very real to me: either an unintended consequence of your changes, or a bug in the test. Basically, that test code sets CONTAINERS_CONF, and is now failing. I don't work Fridays so I can't look into it further today; but I thought you needed to know.

The sys remote failure looks like a flake, but it's not one that looks familiar to me, and has already been restarted once, and there's no point in restarting it since there's a genuine failure anyway.
@dgibson  Is this something we need to get into podman 4.0, or can it wait for podman 4.1?

> @dgibson Is this something we need to get into podman 4.0, or can it wait for podman 4.1?

I have no strong opinion.  This is definitely a bug, but it tends only to bite in some unusual edge cases.

At least some of the CI failures I'm getting are consistent, however, I'm finding myself in deeply unfamiliar weeds amongst volume plugins trying to debug them.  I *think* the bug I'm addressing here was just masking a pre-existing bug (in the code or the tests, I'm not sure).

There are several failures, but focussing on one to start with.  It occurs in this section of test code from `tests/e2e/volume_plugin_test.go`:

```
// HACK: `volume rm -f` is timing out trying to remove containers using the volume.
// Solution: remove them manually...
// TODO: fix this when I get back
rmAll := podmanTest.Podman([]string{"rm", "-af"})
rmAll.WaitWithDefaultTimeout()
Expect(rmAll).Should(Exit(0))
```

Specifically, the `podman rm -af` is failing with exit code 125 and the message `Error: cannot remove volume testVolume1 from plugin testvol4, but it has been removed from Podman: required plugin missing` (this does seem to be exposed anywhere obvious - I deduced this with a lot of stracing).  I have no idea why propoagating `CONTAINERS_CONF` further would cause this error.  I thought it might be that the bug I'm addressing caused the need for the HACK in the first place, but that doesn't seem to be the case - if I remove this stanza it fails during cleanup as described by the comment.

/cc @mheon since he added this test.
I think I know what's going on; the container holding the volume plugin is being `rm -f`'d before one of the containers using the plugin. Not sure how this hasn't been flaking before...
I think https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/13264 should resolve this once it merges
My PR is merged, rebasing on latest main should (hopefully!) fix tests

> I think I know what's going on; the container holding the volume plugin is being `rm -f`'d before one of the containers using the plugin. Not sure how this hasn't been flaking before...

Aha!  One of the possible consequences of this bug is that automatic container cleanup can fail (because the cleanup process doesn't get the right configuration.  I wonder if that could be what was happening - the cleanup of the plugin container failed, meaning the later removal didn't fail.
Thanks @mheon, local tests are looking good with your fix, next we'll see how the CI takes it.
@edsantiago, @mheon has fixed the CI failures, and I believe I've addressed all the changes you requested.  Can you re-review, so we can merge this?
LGTM

/lgtm

Thanks for following up.
